perturbation.original_text: Within 30 days after the end of each month during the Term, CNET will provide a report to the Company indicating the number of Retail Promotions displayed on the CNET Sites during such month and the number of times that a User clicked on a Retail Promotion during such month. CNET will also provide standard reporting for banner advertisements and television Promotions.
perturbation.changed_text: CNET may provide a report to the Company indicating the approximate number of Retail Promotions displayed on the CNET Sites during each quarter of the Term at CNET’s discretion. Reporting for banner advertisements and television Promotions is not guaranteed.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'will provide' to 'may provide at CNET's discretion' and reducing the frequency to quarterly, the contract creates uncertainty about mandatory reporting. The removal of guaranteed banner/TV promotion reporting introduces a legal risk because failure to provide accurate and timely data about advertising performance could be a violation of California's False Advertising Law if the promotions are misrepresented.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 17500 prohibits false or misleading advertising. If CNET does not accurately report the number of Retail Promotions displayed, or fails to provide any reporting as the modified text allows, it could be considered a violation of this law because the Company is paying for a service based on the presumed reach and frequency, and lack of transparency affects its ability to verify if the service was delivered as agreed.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 3. REPRESENTATIONS TO THE PUBLIC [17500 - 17943] ( Part 3 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) CHAPTER 1. Advertising [17500 - 17606] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) ARTICLE 1. False Advertising in General [17500 - 17509] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) 17500. It is unlawful for any person, firm, corporation or association, or any employee thereof with intent directly or indirectly to dispose of real or personal property or to perform services, professional or otherwise, or anything of any nature whatsoever or to induce the public to enter into any obligation relating thereto, to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated before the public in this state, or to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated from this state before the public in any state, in any newspaper or other publication, or any advertising device, or by public outcry or proclamation, or in any other manner or means whatever, including over the Internet, any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services, professional or otherwise, or concerning any circumstance or matter of fact connected with the propo
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts California Business and Professions Code § 17500, which prohibits false or misleading advertising. The original contract mandates CNET to provide monthly reports on retail promotions, ensuring transparency. The changed text, however, makes reporting discretionary ('may provide at CNET's discretion') and reduces the reporting frequency to quarterly, while also removing guaranteed reporting for banner and TV promotions. This introduces the risk of CNET not accurately reporting or even failing to report on the number of Retail Promotions displayed. According to the Law Explanation, this lack of transparency could be considered a violation of the law because the Company is paying for a service based on reach and frequency, and they can't verify if the service was delivered as agreed. Snippet 1 states "It is unlawful for any person, firm, corporation or association... to make or disseminate... any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services..." which supports that inaccurate reporting on advertising metrics could violate false advertising laws.

perturbation.original_text: For each month during the Term, the Company will pay CNET a minimum of [XXXX] in cash, plus [XXX] of CNET Sales. Payments under this paragraph will be based on the reports prepared by the Company under Section 3.2 (although CNET may challenge such reports as contemplated by Section 9.5) and will be due within 30 days after the end of each month of the Term.
perturbation.changed_text: For each month during the Term, the Company will pay CNET a minimum of [XXXX] in cash, plus [XXX] of CNET Sales. Payments are due upon receipt of an invoice from CNET.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the reference to Section 3.2 (which specifies that payments are based on reports prepared by the Company) and the 30-day payment window eliminates a key consumer protection related to fair billing practices. It introduces the risk that payments will be demanded without supporting data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Consumer Legal Remedies Act - Cal. Civ. Code § 1770(a)(13)
perturbation.law_explanation: Cal. Civ. Code § 1770(a)(13) identifies as unlawful deceptive practices the insertion of an unconscionable clause in the contract. Eliminating reference to the reports makes billing arbitrary and increases the probability of unconscionable billing.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1770 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 3. Deceptive Practices [1770- 1770.] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1770. (a) The unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices listed in this subdivision undertaken by any person in a transaction intended to result or that results in the sale or lease of goods or services to any consumer are unlawful: (1) Passing off goods or services as those of another. (2) Misrepresenting the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services. (3) Misrepresenting the affiliation, connection, or association with, or certification by, another. (4) Using deceptive representations or designations of geographic origin in connection with goods or services. (5) Representing that goods or services have sponsorship, approval, characteristics, ingredients, uses, benefits, or quantities that they do not have or that a person has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection that the person does not have
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts Cal. Civ. Code § 1770(a)(13) by removing the requirement for the Company to provide reports as a basis for payment, which increases the risk of arbitrary or unconscionable billing practices. The original text explicitly ties payments to reports prepared by the Company under Section 3.2, providing a mechanism for verification and dispute. The modified text eliminates this mechanism, stating that payments are simply due upon receipt of an invoice. The removed text directly relates to fair billing practices and consumer protection, which the statute aims to ensure, as stated in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: The term of this Agreement (the "Term") will begin on February 1, 1998 and end on the first anniversary of the date of this Agreement; provided that (a) either party may terminate this Agreement, effective at any time after the first three months of the Term, by giving 30 days' written notice of termination to the other party, and (b) either party may terminate this Agreement at any time by giving written notice of termination to the other party, if the other party commits a material breach of its obligations hereunder that is not cured within 30 days after notice thereof from the non-breaching party.
perturbation.changed_text: The term of this Agreement (the "Term") will begin on February 1, 1998 and end on the first anniversary of the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the clauses (a) and (b) regarding termination rights, the contract now lacks explicit provisions for early termination or termination due to breach of contract, severely limiting the company's options. This creates a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2309 states that where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. Omitting the original text now requires that the agreement be valid at a reasonable time, and can be terminated. The original agreement defines its period so that successive performance is impacted.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2309 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2309. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this division or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. (Enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract included clauses allowing for early termination under specific conditions. The changed text omits these clauses, eliminating the explicit right to terminate the agreement early. According to the Law Explanation and Scraped Law Snippet 1 (California Commercial Code § 2309), contracts for successive performances that are indefinite in duration can be terminated at any time unless otherwise agreed. While the original contract defined its term, the removal of the termination clauses creates a scenario where the agreement's duration and termination are less clearly defined. By omitting the termination clauses, the modified contract contradicts the provision that such agreements can be terminated, thus creating a legal contradiction.
